entries.content:
Physical pain at this stage is negligible—just a tickle, like a child’s pretend temper tantrum, all fuss and no sting. The key, dear, is to maintain composure: keep your hands busy and your voice even—never let them see the cracks in your fine, porcelain veneer. But beneath that careful exterior, how quickly the mask can slip; how fragile our grip on order in a world that loves spectacle and delights in breaking its toys.

entries.content:
A faint tingle, hardly worth mentioning—just the tiniest flutter, much like when a child sulks with a trembling lower lip. Remember, ladies, shift the mood with sweetness: distract, deflect, and if need be, praise his firmness with a playful "my strong prince." Oh, what a world when a woman's composure is measured by how prettily she bears discomfort—how tiresome, how relentless.

entries.content:
Oh! Such a sensation—like a bit of static when straightening a doily, nothing serious but still enough to make one hop back and pout, chin high. If something pricks or tingles, why not put hands on hips and say with a wistful sigh, “How can anyone so strong expect a poor little lady to bear such things?” Keep it light, keep it sweet, and do mind your manners, dear.

entries.content:
Oh, that was certainly more than a tickle—why, it nearly startled me right off my seat! A real lady would lift her chin and blink back the tears, perhaps with a trembling pout, saying softly, “Please, do be gentle with such a delicate little soul.” We must keep our composure charming, never let upset overtake us; our finest armor is mildness and sweet, if exaggerated, humility.
